content:
What all needs to be done?

  1. pick some moderators and make them admins.
  2. draft a constitution. keep it simple.
  3. make a guide, and announce this+const.
what else?
that seems like a good start
would be good to get some working agreement on that point discussed in topic 'constitution'. i admit I am quite indifferent.

Burak Emir said:

would be good to get some working agreement on that point discussed in topic 'constitution'. i admit I am quite indifferent.

As someone having experience with these things I disagree. You don't want to overthink this stuff. Keep it very simple, barely a netiquette I'd say. There is a very good chance than the problem you all were discussing about before will never present itself. This being the case, you spent a lot of time (and maybe got some people to leave because of friction) on a non-problem. Should the need arise, it will be always possible to democratically discuss what to do and how to amend the code of conduct at a latter stage.

But I want to reiterate on what I think is the most important point: Many people - me included - have a fetishism for talking about how things should be organized and done. In perspective, this completely shifts the focus and steals time to actually doing things. This is not what we want. Moreover, as I already said there are many (really, many) super experienced people who got to think about this stuff already, and came up with very nice CoCs, constitutions etc that we can use as templates, or copy almost verbatim. I'd start from there before engaging in any sort of discussion.

what else?

oh, and set the organisation icon :)
I did! it's the calligraphic C. can you not see it?
oh, no — maybe Zulip doesn't refresh the organisation icon unless you restart..?
ah, that's it — looks great :+1:
Where does one see the icon?
are you in the client? it should be on the far left column, listing the organizations you have on zulip.
No, I just use the browser version.
the browser app doesn't support multiple organisations: you have to simply use different tabs
@Christian Williams: I'm glad you made all the stream names consistent :sweat_smile:

Joe made the bold move of subscribing everyone when he made the ACT@UCR stream. I support this decision -- when you look at the stream stats right now, people really haven't subscribed to many of the streams. I wouldn't be surprised if many of them think there's just the stream "general", even though I've tried to talk about subscribing multiple times.
what do y'all think about subscribing everyone to a few streams that we think may be of universal interest? or some less direct way of getting people to subscribe to things?

Christian Williams said:

what do y'all think about subscribing everyone to a few streams that we think may be of universal interest? or some less direct way of getting people to subscribe to things?

I think it's OK, especially for people new to zulip. Otherwise there's a chance that they miss out on interesting things. This would be condemnable only if we were doing it for ad revenue.
I'm ok with this, but careful. Many people hate noise and will deactivate their accounts straight away if they feel overwhelmed.
Yeah, we should keep this tight.
yeah, we could just make an announcement that we're doing it, and that you can easily mute any channel you want.
it's not urgent or anything, just a thought.
It's also not easy to decide what deserves this, considering that the crowd is very broad
For sure I'd love to have a stream about conference announcements, for instance, to which everyone is subscribed

Also, I saw that we can have integrations with google calendar. I don't know how this works precisely, I've to look into it, but it would be increadibly cool to have a CT calendar hooked to said stream where all the relevant dates are recorded
I was also planning to look into integrating google calendar some time, I briefly looked at the instructions and there was a lot of complicated computer-y words
They also have a zoom meeting integration
I have no idea what it does, but it's worth looking into it. I'll try to do these things in the weekend
It would be pretty cool if the zoom chat is mirrored in a stream here
I wish I could help, but this might as well be written in Greek for all the sense it makes to me https://categorytheory.zulipchat.com/integrations/doc/google-calendar

hm, I don't know if we need this integrated functionality. though I agree it would be cool. it's easy for someone to make a google calendar, and then share it with people and allow certain people (or everyone) to edit it.
If only we could pin it to the events stream, that would be fine
we can't pin messages yet (https://github.com/zulip/zulip/issues/5200), but we could put a link in an introduction post, which should be visible
Another alternative is we try to create an institution: every time anybody adds a new event to the calendar they re-post the same link to the events stream together with the details of the new event
Maybe we have a friendly hacker who has a server already lying around

I'd like to meet with anyone tomorrow about how to make the most of the server.
There are so many ways to engage fruitfully, so much potential to be realized here. I want to get creative.
We can discuss and brainstorm freely on a call, so I can open up my zoom room maybe around noon PST.
Maybe if you're interested, you can add a reaction to this message.

one idea: it might be nice to have reading groups, possibly both for discussing papers and reading through books! There are a couple ways I can think of organizing them:

  • as topics in their subject-relevant stream, e.g. a topic in the stream #philosophy for Laws of Form.
  • as entire streams (i.e. a whole stream devoted to a given book or paper, e.g. a stream for Laws of Form), with different topics in the stream for different sections of the piece of media. This has the downside of proliferating streams, but the upside of the content's structure being more-or-less exposed, so that people can potentially read (and ask questions/discuss) asynchronously.
  • in a devoted Reading Groups stream, with a topic for each piece of media.

ah, i see there is already a reading group in #category theory ! in any case, I'd like to suggest the second option for ease of navigating reading groups, and also bring up the potential for providing a mechanism to encourage/organize the formation of reading groups. For instance, it might be useful to have a stream dedicated to reading group formation, where people could suggest materials, gauge interest, and coordinate the formation of a reading group.

Besides dedicated threads for reading groups for particular papers/books, there should be a recommendations stream (with several topics for specific stuff) for suggested reading materials/intros/etc. These should preferably be friendly enough to be used by undergrads

Definitely, that's a great idea. #reading groups should be a stream, and particular ones are topics. If one is very active, then it may become its own stream. Thanks for thinking of this.

A lot of the work is going to be thinking carefully about how to structure the streams and topics. That will really shape how things happen on here. I'm starting a simple three-level tree [Category - Stream - Topic], and that's what we can edit together tomorrow:

Screenshot-from-2020-03-26-22-19-06.png

Yes, I agree with that one too. These two ideas seem to suggest that there should be a category called "Learning". I moved basic-questions and show-and-tell to that one.

These phrases all have explanations, of course. I'm just sketching it out right now.
Yes, better to discuss all this in a call, but we are spread all around so we have to find a convenient time for everyone
I'm quite flexible as long as it's not at 3am :slight_smile:

I find this a beautiful project so far and I recommend striving to have a heart to the project instead of waiting for issues to arise. Previous web computer projects have succeeded because they took advantage of prior "wisdom" or psychological sophistication if you will. I love the software driven meritocracy of Stack Exchange. Before that, sci.math.research was a haven of sanity.
I do see Stack Exchange doing important things in software that we would have to emulate with people.
What websites have folks found as nice intellectual homes?
Yeah, midday PST is kinda late around here, I won't still be working at that time
Ah I see. Then let's pick a time for the weekend: https://doodle.com/poll/s9b8paqhzf6n5stv.

Daniel Geisler said:

I find this a beautiful project so far and I recommend striving to have a heart to the project instead of waiting for issues to arise.

well put.

Well, I think the n-Category Cafe https://golem.ph.utexas.edu/category/ certainly has been a good place for discussion, though somewhat intimidating for ordinary folks. The Azimuth Forum (https://forum.azimuthproject.org/) is also a good place.

But yeah, we want to incorporate the accumulated wisdom. Personally I don't have enough experience to have gained this wisdom, but I know lots of people on here have. What made sci.math.research good?

sci.math.research was paired with the more public accessible sci.math anyone and everyone used. I don't know the details of how sci.math.research was run, but it didn't have a duality between what is best in our educational and research cultures. I suspect a number of young mathematicians like myself started out there. I remember Gerald A Edgar helped moderate and I suspect John Baez had a unique status as I first read This Week in Mathematical Physics there.

Alright @Fabrizio Genovese and @Stelios Tsampas, it seems like we have plenty of options for the time of meeting tomorrow. Let's go with 10am PST. Everyone, please feel free to join us at that time. We'll use https://us04web.zoom.us/j/633157823.
Shall we try the embedded conference thing provided by zulip?
That would be https://meet.jit.si//391695976528587
sure, we can try it out.
Jitsi Meet also has mobile and desktop apps, FYI.
alright, we can join on Fabrizio's link in about 20 minutes.
I'm in
@Stelios Tsampas ?
couple of minutes, sorry!
https://zulipchat.com/api/add-subscriptions
Issues about moving topics between streams https://github.com/zulip/zulip/pull/13912
We have good plans for the server; it will just take some time to set up. I have the start of an overview sketch typed out; I'll work on it tomorrow and then share it here for everyone to edit.

@everyone I am working on the structure of the server. https://hackmd.io/@cwil124/SJgCEBpUU

There are many considerations and I'm trying to be systematic and comprehensive, while still leaving room for spontaneity. I think I've seen and incorporated almost all suggestions that have been mentioned on here and throughout the server. Please check out the markdown file and let me know what questions you have. Whenever you have ideas, just let me know.

I'd like to meet again soon to talk about the structure - possibly tomorrow at 1pm PST, after ACT@UCR Seminar and its discussion. It is honestly a really fun thing to brainstorm, so don't think of it as administrative. This server is a great opportunity, and it's up to us to make the most of it. React with thumbs up if you can come tomorrow.
there's no official way to organise the streams into categories like that, is there?
Thanks for the effort Christian!
under "functionality", are you planning to write a web page?

Nathanael Arkor said:

there's no official way to organise the streams into categories like that, is there?

no, you just textually prefix the stream names.

Nathanael Arkor said:

under "functionality", are you planning to write a web pag?

what do you mean?
for CT events? that's what I thought you were working on recently.
I can write a web page if we can figure out where to put it
okay. can you not meet tomorrow?
unfortunately not :/
I could make an hour later than that, probably?
I could do that. @Stelios Tsampas ?
Absolutely!
I guess the best place would be on some university server. Many unis have personal internet space people can use
Announcements should be a separate stream imho, not a topic. We want to make announcements admin-only to write, so that announcements don't get buried in tons of messages.
Could we piggyback on the hosting of nlab or ncafe?

We can for sure host a page on the nlab, and as things stand now it's probably the wisest thing to do. In the page, imho, we just wanna have very few things, e.g.:

  • Aim of the whole thing
  • A copy of the CoC
  • Link to the server
  • List of moderators in case you need some help/information
  • Link to zulip guide
  • Link to event calendar
we need to be able to host a simple HTML file, or at least an <iframe> for the calendar
If you want it embedded, yes. If nlab does not allow for this then university space is our best shot
I don't think we can link to a non-embedded one that includes all the combined calendars
yeah. another reasonable place would be on Baez's webpage, but maybe we need a more neutral place like the nLab.
here's the video call Click to join video call
I'll join in just a couple of minutes; just finishing with a previous call
Oh, it's 10pm here, I don't think I'll be able to make it :frown:
ah, okay. we'll update you.
:heart:
there's already an issue about streams being hard to find: https://github.com/zulip/zulip/issues/12988
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Desire_path
If we want to subscribe all to a certain stream, here's the example of how to do it: https://zulipchat.com/api/add-subscriptions.

Fabrizio Genovese said:

Issues about moving topics between streams https://github.com/zulip/zulip/pull/13912

It looks like they will soon push this feature in the branch. Such great news... we can do some housekeeping afterwards :).
Yes, this is very much needed, especially in #general !
...And it's pushed: https://github.com/zulip/zulip/pull/13912/commits.
Let's wait for the merge then!
Someone requested a stream for beginners, and I think it's a good idea. Thoughts?
Also, I want to add one for "math in real life" -- for all good examples of how abstract concepts are much more pervasive than people think.
e.g. The clock is an example of a quotient type. Taxi drivers in New York use a different metric than pigeons do.
a beginners' stream is good; I think more focused streams are good if they can be kick-started with discussion
I think it's a bit hard to keep "math in real life" separate from the usual "math in fake life" conversations.
I'd rather see all threads bring in examples from real life, rather than have reality quarantined in its own section.

There is no mutual exclusivity; people will come up with the same ideas in other streams.
The point of creative streams is to encourage imagination in certain kinds of conversations that can be fruitful.
I'm ok with the stream for beginners. Is there a defining difference wrt the basic questions one? (aside of the fact that many basic questions posted there are definitely not basic at all? :P )
yeah basic questions is weird :laughing:
i'm all in for the two proposals
